item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember first reading this due to the random post finder and the SCP-701 reference blowing my mind. Now that I have this account, enjoy your +1.

Edit: And like, the million other references were pretty insane as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are so many things good with this skip. I could probably write a book about it.

This skip crosslinks half a dozen other SCPs and bodies of lore together, without feeling forced in any way. The exploration logs are some of the best I've read on the site, and it uses an unconventional format I find greatly superior to the conventional format. Like all of Metaphysician's works, it has excellent hooking potential and narrative construction.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is, it's called a bookmark. It is a standard browser feature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the coolest ones I've seen. Why isn't there a thing where you can favorite articles so you don't have to search the whole thing to find them if you want to read them again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are many of them:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Henry_Percy,_9th_Earl_of_Northumberland

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the balance between professionalism and what we can see of Narváez's personality in the exploration log. It definitely adds something to see that there is an actual person experiencing this. Same with the interview. That kind of total violence doesn't usually strike a chord with me, but the descriptions here and the implication that those descriptions were planted did make me uncomfortable. Actually, I think that everything about the planted description, being so beautifully described but about something so awful, is just fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't believe I missed this one up until now. One of the most perfectly Lovecraftian entries I've ever seen(so many people try for Lovecraft and fail so I appreciate it when it's done right!). I'd like to see one more article that expands this further, but that's about as far as I'd want it to go - the Foundation can't know THAT much about all this or it loses its mystique. Tales can explore it beyond that point. Thanks for a marvelous read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The original, real Henry Percy was actually the sixth earl of Northumberland, not the ninth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Disclaimer: I haven't read the other comments here; I'm just replying because I voted)

So I have issues with the tone used in the interview log; the dialogue doesn't feel realistic in any way starting from when he starts recounting the trip into the "other" city. That said, it sort of feels like that was your point; that he's speaking from a place of pure nightmare-world insanity and it starts coming out poetic even though he doesn't mean for it to, as that seems to sort of be a theme here (starting with the first written recount and the in-universe critique thereof). It feels very Lovecraftian.

All in all, honestly? I really like this one. It's interesting, it has a story to tell, and it has plenty of potential to spawn further stories, which is something I personally find really valuable in a skip. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man is weird seeing "their" used like that.

Agent Papadopoulos was found to be in critical condition upon their exit of SCp…..

Super amazing read. Like a twisted neverworld (more so lol (

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed. Can't believe I never noticed that mistake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the interview with Papadopoulos, Papa's second-last entry didn't have his name, making it feel like it was carrying on from the doctor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did not think the foundation had a alchemist. Cool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
>A Foundation alchemist was consulted

It's past 2AM and I nearly swore out loud when I hit that line.

This is a good read, a unique alternate-space, and the exploration logs are on-point. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, will fix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First collapsible:

"[…] does not entirely confirm to the physical rules we are used to."

I suspect that should be 'conform', not 'confirm'?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The King in Yellow itself was inspired by Poe's Red Death

Huh, I didn't know that. Maybe it's because I read them so long ago, but I don't see much resemblance between the two…

I also used "Red Death" to refer to a Sarkic bioweapon

Do you mean the mass of flesh mentioned at the end of SCP-2075?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes Hercules! Totally forgot. That is true - all part of that borrowing game really common to the weird and gothic genres.

The King in Yellow itself was inspired by Poe's Red Death (from The Masque of the Red Death). I also used "Red Death" to refer to a Sarkic bioweapon (which many assumed as the Flesh that Hates but was never my intention, heh).

And thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I recall correctly, Ambrose Bierce first came up with Carcosa, but I could be wrong. The King in Yellow, though, was definitely Chambers' creation.

Love this skip, BTW. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, Carcosa and The King in Yellow predate Lovecraft and were created by Robert Chambers (but, was adopted by Lovecraft, which was a fairly common practice in weird fiction at the time). This is derivative in the sense that it is meant to be - an homage to Chambers. The original Hanged King SCP was derivative of the play The King in Yellow (as presented in the writings of Chambers). At the same time, Carcosa is never touched on in any major detail. As I've wanted to do something inspired by Chambers, I decided to work within the Foundationverse and use their equivalent of the Yellow King and Carcosa.

While I know that the last words of the agent are basically burned into his mind and he is repeating something, I don't want to use quotes because while *I* as the author know that, the Foundation really doesn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm feeling neutral here. Good "exploration" SCPs are a treasure, and I'm liking what I've read thus far of Metaphysician's work (I'm going through the whole archive in order) but a couple of things are bugging me.

First: The interview formatting issue as pointed out by Wuz a few posts up. If this was intentional, as a stylistic thing, leaving the dialogue unattributed would've worked better for the agent's next statement, which was clearly not intended to be his own words.

Second: It's rather derivative of Carcosa, as it appears in the Cthulhu Mythos. As the Hanged King is a spin on the King in Yellow, creating an associated setting that continues to resemble that work is a little bit on the nose.

Neither of these gripes make it worthy of a downvote, but they stand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. That is an improvement!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would +1 this if I could. The one and only thing that stops me from doing so is my intense cringe at the supposed Elizabethan English in the period documents — in the event of a revision, I can offer my services.

Just by way of example...
click to hide

Here's the letter to Marlowe revised. Just a few minor edits, but to an anal-retentive fuckwad like me they make all the difference.

To my singular goode Friend, may this Missive reach thee ere 'tis too late.

'Twas thou who urg'd against my building of the Janus Gate. My Insult was cruell, having deem'd thee foolish and ignorant of the Sciences: I prithee forgive mine Arrogance.

'Twas thou who suffer'd the Evil to which I was blind. I show'd thee the Other Ordinary, and allow'd the secret Darknesse to coil around the Cinder of thy beautiful Heart. I was blind, but now I see.

I beseech thee to burn that accurs'd Play and return it to Ashe. Thy Patron seeks to corrupt and defile: Whence He cometh, there are Things that simply should not be. The Ambassador shall exploit thee, as they did us. I have seal'd the Janus Gate so that only the Enlighten'd may enter. May they have the Wisdom to see what I coulde not, and the Power to slay the wretch'd King within.

Damn that Metropolis of Blood, that terrible Realm and its antient countless Crimes. Consign thy Play to the Fire, deny thy vile Patron, and aroint thee from this Madnesse. We would fain welcome thee backe into the Night.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There was one part in the interview that looked like Dr. Narang was talking when it's actually Agent Papadopoulos talking. I am not sure if I am just reading this the wrong way, but is this intentional?

This is the part:

Dr. Laxmi Narang: Please go on.

I watched the ceiling move as it dragged my broken body from room to room. Eventually we stopped and it lifted me into the air, held me up before the throne. There I saw the King. It was anchored in place, hallowed bonds around its corpse-like hands and throat - its face hidden beneath a veil.

Impish creatures crawled all over it, caressing its twitching body as if intending comfort while others pulled the tethers even tighter. The King trembled and quivered and I saw pale tendrils slither in and out of its tattered robes. I looked on as the veil was lifted… [a change in tone suggesting lucidity] I want to die. I can't live with what I've done. Please kill me. End this. I can't feel my legs. I can't feel my arms. Not like this. Not like this. Please, I'm begging you…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder what happens if someone enters SCP-2264 with a tattoo of any other color than one of the "accepted" ones? Does it change color? Does it dissappear entirely? And does this reverse after leaving Alagadda? Aldo, is SCP-701-1 supposed to be the Ambassador or the King?

This is one of my favourite SCPs on the entire site. It's just so atmospheric.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Ambassador was recently in Adytum. That's about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is Sarkic tagged here? Admittedly I have limited knowledge on Sarkic so it's highly likely I missed the subtle connection to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, thanks. The problem with the masked lords was the order of certain sentences and a few words, so fixed that and working on other things now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really enjoyed this. Derivative, but derivative in the best way.

(I really don't mean "derivative" as a downside, even though it often is when people point it out; I think the Foundation, as a freely open creative writing site in a coherent collaborative universe that is either public domain or creative commons, is the right place for a certain kind of derivative work, and this is the kind of derivative work that I myself have written elsewhere on this site. And sometimes this critique is used incorrectly — for instance, to critique the original Hanged King SCP. Of course it's derivative! And only one of Shakespeare's plays had a plot he came up with himself. Doesn't mean they weren't brilliant.)

The ending interview pays off well; I was a bit 'eh' on the torture aspects because I've seen it so much before, but the actual Ambassador and King were memorable and terrifying.

The Marlowe prologue is just okay. It could be cut down, though (or something added to make it more interesting; the interviews each pull their own weight and then some, but the Marlowe prologue doesn't quite).

Overall, very good. +1'd

Time for some nitpicks.
– hide block

Operatives are to be reassigned and replaced monthly due to the threat of psychological addiction to SCP-2264-B.

This can be repeated here, but belongs in the containment procedures as well.

This section:

I have seen the Masked Lords, always at a distance, all except the Wearer of the Anguished Mask. I was informed that they were the victim of a political struggle some time ago (the reason never known, if reason existed at all) and cast into some dreadful dimensional backwater. It would only be a matter of time before they returned. They were said to be the chief advisors to the King of Alagadda.

It's unclear whether all the masked lords were cast out, or just the Wearer of the Anguished Mask. If all the masked lords, it should read:

I have seen the Masked Lords, always at a distance, all except the Wearer of the Anguished Mask. I was informed that they were the victims of a political struggle some time ago (the reason never known, if reason existed at all) and cast into some dreadful dimensional backwater. It would only be a matter of time before they returned. They were said to be the chief advisors to the King of Alagadda.

[And additionally, some special mention should be made of why the Wearer of the Anguished Mask has been apparently cast out further than the other Masked Lords.]

If the text is meant to refer to the Wearer of the Anguished Mask, it should read:

I have seen the Masked Lords, always at a distance, all except the Wearer of the Anguished Mask. I was informed that the Wearer of the Anguished Mask was the victim of a political struggle some time ago (the reason never known, if reason existed at all) and cast into some dreadful dimensional backwater. It would only be a matter of time before they returned. They were said to be the chief advisor to the King of Alagadda.

The part in italics is just a suggestion to help with clarity; no change is necessarily required in this case. However, the latter change would be required grammatically.

I'm not making the above changes myself because I'm unsure which was intended, though I suspect the latter.

I was gonna complain about the tone of the first report, but "although it has been requested that he use a more professional tone with regards to his reports." fixed that for me (and made me laugh). So no issue there.

I do have a complaint about the end of the final report: there's no reason for the paragraph breaks except dramatic pause. And it's also overplayed: your prose has drama enough without separating both those sentences out on their own lines.

I'd recommend the Agent pausing for a long time before the last two sentences, then the interviewer prompting him to continue, then having him say the last two sentences (on the same line). Or something like that. Would get across the dramatic pause while retaining flow.

– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What an absolutely fantastic SCP. The story, the writing, the..EVERYTHING.
The pacing was very good, it didn't feel like it was dragging and it didn't rush. The story kept me on edge the entire time, and the part where they met the Ambassador and king? Terrifying.
Also, for some reason this SCP reminded me of "The King In Yellow" by Robert W. Chambers. And considering that it's one of my favourite books, that's definitely a good thing.
Keep up the good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
701 has been one of my favourite skips for a long time, and ive really been looking for a great expansion like this one, so good work! +1

one small thing though: i have always been under the impression that the entity in the pic for 701 was the hanged king, and that the ambassador was separate. either way, excellent job

EDIT: upon reread of some 701 stuff, it seems i was wrong

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I´m amazed at the originality and complexity in this. All of the mentions towards people living in the Victorian age feel so real, like i could research some more and find out they actually existed. Amazing article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I haven't read a single other article on the site that captures the faintly ominous dream - tone of lovecraft so well. Both the worldbuilding and the immersion were sublime.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From my understanding, the colours of red, yellow, black and white are those of the Four Humours? Blood, yellow bile, black bile and phlegm respectively.

The humours are referenced in Elizabethan works, which is likely why it could be relevant here. I could be wrong though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Such an elaborate tale of an impossible world, brings me serious nostalgia of reading tacky wild fantasy novels when I was younger. A story that creates an engaging environment like this is bound to drag the reader in by their ankles into it's magnificent universe.

I hope this article receives more attention, it is absolutely cosmic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know, I've been reading through the author's page. Wow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit man, this was really great. I mean it, I haven't been hooked in an article this much for a while. The easiest +1 I've ever given, and this goes right up to my top favorites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I will do that. There was so much I wanted to show but wanted to keep the dream state as it was, where detail was difficult.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You could always explore the roleplaying aspect in a tale. :I That'd be cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In Alagadda, everyone must play a role. I believe the Foundation disrupted that and it was just a matter of time before the Ambassador would punish them. In retrospect, I wish I explored that role playing more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your ideas worked, it is very good. It feels like Carcosa but… different. The dream logic way you described it was similar to the way Carcosa was described in the second DG book in a chapter by John Tynes (that's intended as a compliment; he is a tremendous writer.) There also happened to be a member of the Delta Green group named Dr. Yu in that book, so it sort of felt like there were enough coincidences there may have been a connection…

Maybe it's just the influence of Alagadda.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Carcosa was a major focus but since, The Hanged King already had the jump on that mythos, I decided to work in my idea with Alagadda. I admit, I don't know Delta Green. Dr. Yu, I don't think they've had a real role yet other than being assistant to a rather eccentric researcher;p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really sounds a lot like Carcosa, right down to the black stars, with a heavy vibe of John Tynes' version of the city from Delta Green: Countdown. (Was Dr. Yu a shoutout, maybe?)

Very cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://fc06.deviantart.net/fs70/f/2015/053/2/e/scp_2264_by_sunnyparallax-d8j1irc.jpg

Some awesome art of Alagadda by Sunnyparallax.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just proves that you can write great things even without connecting them to your burgeoning Sarkic Cult canon. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddamnit, Metaphysician. When the article namedropped Alagadda (which was supercool in its own right), I idly thought "Are we going to see Adytum next?" Quit screwing with my head. :P

Digging the worldbuilding here for sure; can't wait to see the next installment in the "Sarkic Metaverse" or whatever the hell I'm going to name it in my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So much worldbuilding so good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Metadoc, y u so epic?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Black Lord, Wearer of the Anguished Mask is a reference to SCP-035, ("Possessive Mask"). The location, Alagadda, is referenced and implied to be the kingdom of the Hanged King in The Hanged King's Tragedy (including the image of the Ambassador of Alagadda). Adytum is a reference to my own little mythos I've tried to develop here - once an ancient city in our world, now somewhere else and much more deadly (its denizens referenced by the Wandsman as smelling of rot and embyriotic fluid; a people obsessed with anomalous manipulation of life and death).These references are meant to show the strange politics of transdimensional entities. The Deathless Merchant of London refers to Mr. Dark of MC&D (he seems the sort to seek a boon). The Wandsman of Kul-Manas is an entity of my own invention, a researcher from another dimension (much like the Foundation) but certainly much more experienced with traveling the multiverse. The confused person the Wandsman references (the one who is stated to have vanished almost as quickly as they came) is SCP-507 ("Reluctant Dimension Hopper").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you explain to me how this is connected with 2480? They both contain the name Adytum and they also contain references to other SCPs that I was told about in the comments. Could you list all of the SCPs that are part of this? I feel like I am missing something.

item.title: This is just. . .wow
item.content:encoded: This is just…SO awesome. Marvelously Lovecraftian while also being reminiscent of the style of writers like Poe. I could not love it more! Would give it a HUNDRED upvotes if I could!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally dislike using "they" like this, but I'll accept it for the Wandsman. For the interview, it's just clunky, i.e. "He is to be restrained for their own protection".
Although I'm still upvoting, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your imagery and worldbuilding are great as always but i just don't think this is the kind of expanding that 701 needs. I don't think it even needs any expanding at all, to be honest. No vote. I don't agree with the concept behind this at all, but I did enjoy a number of elements in this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My twopence? Drop it. You didn't crosslink to SCP-2217 or SCP-610 when writing your other parts of this mythos, with Ion and all that. I don't deem the crosslink necessary. That being said, it's not a bad crosslink or anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you!:x I admit, the log part was hard - melodramatic (though, what they went to would do that). Keep in mind that his last lines are not necessarily his own words but the message he was kept alive to deliver. Thus the change in tone and style. I feel in the end that I should have approached that differently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meta, you're my favorite author. You write everything I wish I could. +1, despite the interview being pretty melodramatic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Haha; I wasn't sure if I should do that or not but I *wanted* people to read 701 so I hid that little link at the end.

I was more torn on that than just about anything.

Would you suggest getting rid of it? I was hoping it would almost blend in. But if it seems too overt, probably should remove it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even so, it doesn't come across that way.

Between that and the frankly unbelievable attempt at period language (in the final letter), I downvoted. Sorry, dude — this is an intriguing piece, but it's got enough language issues to keep throwing me out of immersion. The connection to 701 seemed pretty neat at first, but it ended up overemphasized. I wish I could like this, but it just doesn't quite make it.

edit: I did giggle aloud when you told the reader to consult an alchemist, though. Well played.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was so excellent that I'm going to upvote in spite of the explicit, unexplained direct link to 701's article. I liked the setup, I loved all of the imagery in it, and I love what it adds to the SCPverse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful and terrifying, take my upvote!
